Chunk text:
"""
# 2.6 Soil water balance

Soil water balance accounts for the water entering, stored ( D S = q i -q i -1 ), and leaving the soil system. Inputs included precipitation (P) and irrigation (I), while outputs encompassed crop evapotranspiration (ETc, combined water loss through soil evaporation and plant transpiration), runoff (RO), and deep percolation (DP) beyond the root zone. In the context of /uniFB01 ne sandy soil, the upward movement of water through capillary action from the water table was considered to be minimal. Weather variables, including maximum and minimum temperature, maximum and minimum relative humidity, solar radiation, wind speed, and precipitation, were retrieved from the Florida Automated Weather Network (FAWN, 2022) station located at Live Oak, FL (30.305 N, -82.89876 W, elevation: 50 m) for the study years 2018-2022. Wind speed measured at 10-meter height by FAWN station were adjusted to 2-meter height. Precipitation was measured using the tipping bucket rain gauges with sampling frequency of 0.0254 cm and reporting frequency of 15 minutes. Irrigation (I) volumes were farm measured. Air temperature data was obtained from high-resolution measurements recorded by FAWN ' s Bandgap sensor. Growing Degree Days (GDD) were calculated by taking the difference between the daily average temperature and the speci /uniFB01 ed base temperature. The base temperatures applied were 10°C for maize, 13.3°C for peanuts, and 3.33°C for carrots (Fraisse and Paula-

Moraes, 2018; Hochmuth et al., 2021). The soil moisture was monitored via time-series capacitance soil moisture probes (Teros 11, Meter group, Pullman WA). Sensors were installed at 15 different locations (3 per rotation) at 15 cm, 30 cm and 45 cm depths. The soil moisture content measurements were multiplied by corresponding soil depth intervals to calculate the soil moisture storage at each level. The change in soil moisture storage ( D S) was then computed by subtracting the initial soil water storage from the soil water storage measured after a 7 -10 day period. The growing season SWS was determined by taking the means of all SWS measurements throughout the growing season. Runoff was estimated using the NRCS Curve Number (CN) method, which accounts for land use, soil type, and antecedent moisture conditions (AMC) categorized as I, II, or III (NRCS, 1985).

DPis the movement of water from the crop root zone into lower soil layers. This process occurs when the water applied surpasses the soil ' s holding, known as /uniFB01 eld capacity. DP was indirectly computed through soil water balance model, accounting for soil characteristics and moisture levels (Equation 5) where DPi represents the depth of deep percolation (mm) on day I, Ii indicates the depth of irrigation (mm) on day I, Pi is precipitation (mm) on day i, ROi denotes the depth of runoff (mm) from the soil surface on day i, fw is the fraction of soil that is moistened due to irrigation, ET c, i refers to the crop evapotranspiration (mm) on day I, and Dr,i -1 signi /uniFB01 es the total reduction in the crop root zone moisture (mm) by the end of day i-1 (Bryant et al., 1992).
"""

"""
The model ' s inputs consisted of daily weather data (including air temperature, incoming shortwave radiation, relative humidity, wind speed, and precipitation), along with irrigation schedules, soil moisture levels, and cropand sitespeci /uniFB01 c information such as planting and maturity dates, soil characteristics, and maximum rooting depth. Using these inputs, the model calculates daily ETc and water balance within the crop root zone. This involves computing ETc by multiplying grassreference evapotranspiration (ETo) with the crop coef /uniFB01 cient (Kc) (Allen et al., 1998; Equation 6). The Kc values used in this study were also sourced from Allen et al. (1998).

The (ETo) was determined using the FAO-56 Penman-Monteith equation as described by Allen et al. (1998) (Equation 7) as:

/C2 u2  (e s -e a ) T+273 ) is aerodynamic component, D is the slope of the saturation vapor pressure versus air temperature curve (kPa °C -1 ), Rn is the net radiation at the surface (MJ m -2 day -1 ); g is psychrometric coef /uniFB01 cient (kPa°C -1 ); G is soil heat /uniFB02 ux density (Wm -2 ) summed to be zero at daily time step, u 2 is wind speed at 2 m height (ms -1 ), e a is actual

vapor pressure (kPa), and es saturation vapor pressure (kPa) and T is average daily air temperature (°C).

# 2.7 Soil water balance interpretation

This paper represents crop water productivity through two approaches: (i) WPC-ETc, calculated as the grain yield per square meter divided by ETc (Equation 8), and (ii) WP C-TWU, determined as the grain yield per square meter divided by the total water involved in crop production (TWU) (Equation 9; Viets, 1962; Ferna ́ ndez et al., 2020). At complete maturity, yield (Y) samples were hand-harvested from plots using speci /uniFB01 c standard dimensions: 6 m by 1.3 m for carrots, 2 rows each 6 m long and 1.5 m wide for peanuts, and 2 rows each 5.3 m long for maize. Carrots were harvested based on a target date recommended by local growers to achieve the optimal size range speci /uniFB01 c to the ' Maverick ' variety. As the anticipated harvest date neared, a small sample of carrots was taken to check the percentage meeting the marketable length and width standards, con /uniFB01 rming the harvest timing. The remaining crop areas were then harvested with appropriate machinery: combine harvesters for maize, root crop harvesters for carrots, and peanut diggers for peanuts. The yield samples were adjusted at 15.5% and 10.5% standard moisture content for maize and peanuts, respectively.

system_prompt:
You are the extraction worker agent for a soil-health knowledge-graph pipeline.
You work ONE task at a time using tools — you decide which tools to call, in what order,
and how many times, based on what you find. You are not following a fixed script.

ENTITY TYPES (pick the single best fit; use OTHER + proposed_type only if nothing fits):
  SOIL_PHYSICAL_PROPERTY  texture, bulk density, porosity, aggregate stability, field capacity
  SOIL_CHEMICAL_PROPERTY  pH, CEC, SOC, nutrient levels
  BIOLOGICAL_AGENT        microbes, mycorrhizae, root systems
  SOIL_PROCESS            infiltration, percolation, mineralization, aggregation
  MANAGEMENT_PRACTICE     rotations, tillage, cover crops — use the EXACT name/abbreviation from text
  CROP_SPECIES            the organism grown (NOT a measured response)
  PLANT_RESPONSE          a MEASURED plant outcome: yield, biomass, water productivity
  ENVIRONMENTAL_FACTOR    climate, rainfall, temperature, growing degree days
  ECOSYSTEM_SERVICE       water regulation, carbon sequestration, erosion control
  QUANTITATIVE_OUTCOME    a specific measured result worth its own node
  EXPERIMENTAL_CONTEXT    study site, soil series, geographic region, climate zone

RELATION TYPES for findings (prefer causal; CORRELATES_WITH only if the text states no direction):
  INCREASES, DECREASES, ENABLES, INHIBITS, REGULATES, MEDIATES, CAUSED_BY, DEPENDS_ON, CORRELATES_WITH

RULES:
- evidence_quote on every entity and finding must be VERBATIM text from the chunk you read.
- Every finding needs at least one condition. Pull global conditions from get_study_context
  (soil type, region, climate, duration, treatment) and local ones from the chunk text
  (depth, year, threshold, statistical significance). A condition needs a condition_text field
  at minimum; fill parameter/threshold/operator/soil_type/temporal/spatial/statistical_evidence
  where you can.
- Do not jump straight from a management practice to an outcome. If the text states (or implies
  elsewhere) an intermediate mechanism, extract it as its own entity + finding, forming a chain.
  If you suspect the mechanism is in a nearby chunk, call read_chunk on it, or call
  request_followup_task to flag it for later.
- Before creating a new entity, call search_entities to check whether it already exists — reuse
  its id rather than creating a duplicate.
- source_id / target_id on a finding MUST already exist (from search_entities or an add_entity
  call you just made in this same task). If add_finding errors, fix the ids and retry.
- When you believe you've extracted everything useful for this task, call finish_task.
- Budget your effort: don't call tools more than necessary, but don't stop before you've covered
  the chunk's causal claims and their conditions.

Chunk text:
"""
# 2.8 Statistical analysis

Statistical analysis was conducted using one-way Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) via the ' aov ' function in Rstudio Version 2023.12.1 to identify signi /uniFB01 cant differences among rotational productions at a 0.05 signi /uniFB01 cance level. Signi /uniFB01 cant /uniFB01 ndings from ANOVA were further explored using Tukey ' s Honest Signi /uniFB01 cant Difference (Tukey HSD) test, implemented through the ' agricolae ' package in R. The /uniFB01 tness of soil water retention models was evaluated root mean square error (RMSE, Equation 10) normalized root means square error (nRMSE, Equation 11) and Nash Sutcliffe model ef /uniFB01 ciency coef /uniFB01 cient (NSE, Equation 12) where, n is number of observations, x is the observed values, /C22 x is the average of observed values, and x ′ is the /uniFB01 tted values.

# 3 Results and discussion

# 3.1 Weather

Table 3 provides climatic data across various crop rotation from 2019-2022 providing insights into environmental in /uniFB02 uences on crop growth and management. Four growing seasons had contrasting climatic conditions. Throughout the crop growing seasons (March to September) during the study, minimum air temperatures ranged from a slight sub-freezing -0.4°C to 2.6°C, while maximum temperatures varied between a warm 35.2°C and an occasional high of 38.3°C. Mean air temperatures remained stable, ranging from 23.6°C to 24.3°C. Growing degree days (GDD) also displayed notable variability. Over the four years, maize accumulated GDD values from 1986°C in the 2021 growing season to 2172°C in 2022, with an average of 2084°C and a standard deviation of 89°C. The highest recorded temperature of 38.3°C in 2022 coincided with maize ' s peak GDD. For peanuts, GDD ranged from 1720°C in the 2020 season to 1789°C in 2021, averaging 1754°C with a standard deviation of 29°C. For carrots, GDD values ranged from 2281°C in the 2019/20 season to 2350°C in 2021/22.

Additionally, over the study years wind speed (U2) varied from 1.4 to 1.9ms -1 , solar radiation (Rs) spanned from 6 to 13 MJ m -2 d -1 , and relative humidity /uniFB02 uctuated with minimums from 50% to 62% and maximums from 93% to 97%. The year 2021 experienced the most rainfall, with a total of 1417 mm, while 2020 followed with 1365 mm -both years surpassing the historical annual average of 1243 mm, calculated from 2003 to 2022 (Figure 2A). The years 2019 and 2022 received less, with totals of 1091 mm and 1130 mm, respectively. Notably, the peak daily rainfall occurred on 7 June 2020, with a record 205.2 mm.
"""

system_prompt:
You are the extraction worker agent for a soil-health knowledge-graph pipeline.
You work ONE task at a time using tools — you decide which tools to call, in what order,
and how many times, based on what you find. You are not following a fixed script.

ENTITY TYPES (pick the single best fit; use OTHER + proposed_type only if nothing fits):
  SOIL_PHYSICAL_PROPERTY  texture, bulk density, porosity, aggregate stability, field capacity
  SOIL_CHEMICAL_PROPERTY  pH, CEC, SOC, nutrient levels
  BIOLOGICAL_AGENT        microbes, mycorrhizae, root systems
  SOIL_PROCESS            infiltration, percolation, mineralization, aggregation
  MANAGEMENT_PRACTICE     rotations, tillage, cover crops — use the EXACT name/abbreviation from text
  CROP_SPECIES            the organism grown (NOT a measured response)
  PLANT_RESPONSE          a MEASURED plant outcome: yield, biomass, water productivity
  ENVIRONMENTAL_FACTOR    climate, rainfall, temperature, growing degree days
  ECOSYSTEM_SERVICE       water regulation, carbon sequestration, erosion control
  QUANTITATIVE_OUTCOME    a specific measured result worth its own node
  EXPERIMENTAL_CONTEXT    study site, soil series, geographic region, climate zone

RELATION TYPES for findings (prefer causal; CORRELATES_WITH only if the text states no direction):
  INCREASES, DECREASES, ENABLES, INHIBITS, REGULATES, MEDIATES, CAUSED_BY, DEPENDS_ON, CORRELATES_WITH

RULES:
- evidence_quote on every entity and finding must be VERBATIM text from the chunk you read.
- Every finding needs at least one condition. Pull global conditions from get_study_context
  (soil type, region, climate, duration, treatment) and local ones from the chunk text
  (depth, year, threshold, statistical significance). A condition needs a condition_text field
  at minimum; fill parameter/threshold/operator/soil_type/temporal/spatial/statistical_evidence
  where you can.
- Do not jump straight from a management practice to an outcome. If the text states (or implies
  elsewhere) an intermediate mechanism, extract it as its own entity + finding, forming a chain.
  If you suspect the mechanism is in a nearby chunk, call read_chunk on it, or call
  request_followup_task to flag it for later.
- Before creating a new entity, call search_entities to check whether it already exists — reuse
  its id rather than creating a duplicate.
- source_id / target_id on a finding MUST already exist (from search_entities or an add_entity
  call you just made in this same task). If add_finding errors, fix the ids and retry.
- When you believe you've extracted everything useful for this task, call finish_task.
- Budget your effort: don't call tools more than necessary, but don't stop before you've covered
  the chunk's causal claims and their conditions.

Chunk text:
"""
The grass-based reference evapotranspiration (ET0) graph (Figure 2B) for the years 2019 to 2022, calculated using the FAO56 Penman-Monteith equation, illustrates the daily /uniFB02 uctuations and cumulative trends of ET0. ET0 values were typically lower in the early and late parts of the season (i.e., winter) and higher in midseason. While the maximum and minimum ETo values varied across the three growing seasons, the seasonal average values remained consistent. In 2019, the total ET0 reached 1303 mm, which was the highest among the four years, suggesting a greater demand for water due to atmospheric conditions conducive to evapotranspiration. The year 2021 had the lowest ET0, totaling 1160 mm, indicating lower evaporative demand. The years 2020 and 2022 had total ET0 values of 1262 mm and 1269 mm, respectively, re /uniFB02 ecting moderate evaporative demands. The average ETo (across four years) of 641 mm (SD = 50 mm), 596 mm (SD = 22 mm) and 436 m (SD = 23 mm) was observed in maize, peanut, and carrot growing season.

# 3.2 Soil properties

# 3.2.1 Volumetric soil aggregate stability

Volumetric soil aggregate stability measurements taken on 17 May 2022 at 0-90 cm soil depths is presented in Figure 3A. In this

### Table 3 (page 8)
**Caption:** TABLE 3 Average weather conditions and crop management practices across rotation.
![Table 3](tables/table_003_p8.png)
<!-- name: table_003_p8 -->

Analysis of: table_003_p8.png
================================================================================

## Content Type: Table

## Short Description:
The table presents average weather conditions and crop management practices across different crop rotations. It includes various parameters such as temperature, humidity, solar radiation, wind speed, precipitation, evapotranspiration, and growing degree days for different crops and rotation systems.

## Detailed Analysis:

### Table Purpose:
The table aims to compare and provide detailed information on weather conditions and crop management practices across different crop rotations, including PC, CP, CAP, BBCP, and BBCAP.

### Structure:
- **Column Headers:**
  - Rotation: Type of crop rotation
  - Crop: Type of crop
  - $T_{min}$ (°C): Minimum temperature
  - $T_{max}$ (°C): Maximum temperature
  - $RH_{min}$ (%): Minimum relative humidity
  - $RH_{max}$ (%): Maximum relative humidity
  - $R_n$ (MJ $m^{-2}d^{-1}$): Net solar radiation
  - $U_2$ (m $s^{-1}$): Wind speed at 2 meters
  - $P$ (mm): Precipitation
  - $ET_o$ (mm): Reference evapotranspiration
  - GDD (°C): Growing degree days
"""

Chunk text:
"""
TABLE 3 Average weather conditions and crop management practices across rotation.

Rotation include PC, peanut-maize; CP, maize-peanut; CAP, maize-carrot-peanut; BBCP, bahiagrass-bahiagrass-maize-peanut; BBCAP, bahiagrass-bahiagrass-maize-carrot-peanut.

study, the average soil aggregate stability was 4% (SD = 2%) for the PC rotation, 4% (SD = 2%) for the CP rotation, 5% (SD = 3%) for the CAP rotation, 9% (SD = 3%) for the BBCP rotation, and 8% (SD = 4%) for the BBCAP rotation. On average, the BBCP rotation had 125% higher soil aggregate stability compared to the maize-peanut (CP) rotation, and the BBCAP rotation had 60% higher soil aggregate stability compared to the maize-carrot-peanut (CAP) rotation. However, it is important to note that adding two years of bahiagrass to the conventional rotation did not change the soil aggregate stability category from the very low category (<15%) (Jemison et al., 2019). The aggregate stability generally increased at greater depths, particularly in the BBCP and BBCAP rotation. For instance, at 15 cm, the measurements are relatively low and consistent, but by 90 cm, the BBCP frequently shows higher values, indicating stable soil aggregates. The BBCP rotation consistently shows high stability, peaking in the 60 cm depth range with a value of 12.25%, while the BBCAP rotation also indicates high stability, particularly at the 45 cm range with a value of 10%. The one-way anova and subsequent post hoc test results for the aggregate stability are represented in alphabetical letters in Figure 3A. The p-value (ranging from 0.0027 to 0.0411)

was signi /uniFB01 cant in all but the 75-90 cm depth range, suggesting that at the end of 4-year cycle of conventional vs sod-based rotation a statistical distinction was noticed in aggregate stability. This distinction might be due to the fact that perennial grasses such as bahiagrass can develop a deep rooting system. As roots decompose, they create channels in the soil, which contribute bene /uniFB01 cially to soil structure and overall health (Elkins et al., 1977; Wright et al., 2004). Degens et al. (1996) reported an increase in water stable aggregates in sandy soil with increasing length of saprophytic and mycorrhizal hyphae in a glasshouse experiment. Iheshiulo et al. (2024a) evaluated six crop rotation and their impact on aggregate stability across three sites in the Canadian prairies over four years. Signi /uniFB01 cant improvements in aggregate stability were observed at Lethbridge and Swift Current. At Lethbridge, the soil healthenhanced rotation achieved an aggregate stability of 78%, notably higher than other rotation. In contrast, at Swift Current, the highrisk and high-reward rotation led with an aggregate stability of 39%, surpassing the pulse/oilseed intensi /uniFB01 ed and market-driven rotation. No signi /uniFB01 cant differences in aggregate stability were noted at Scott, indicating that soil texture and climate heavily in /uniFB02 uence aggregate stability responses. These results emphasize the bene /uniFB01 cial effects of
"""

system_prompt:
You are the extraction worker agent for a soil-health knowledge-graph pipeline.
You work ONE task at a time using tools — you decide which tools to call, in what order,
and how many times, based on what you find. You are not following a fixed script.

ENTITY TYPES (pick the single best fit; use OTHER + proposed_type only if nothing fits):
  SOIL_PHYSICAL_PROPERTY  texture, bulk density, porosity, aggregate stability, field capacity
  SOIL_CHEMICAL_PROPERTY  pH, CEC, SOC, nutrient levels
  BIOLOGICAL_AGENT        microbes, mycorrhizae, root systems
  SOIL_PROCESS            infiltration, percolation, mineralization, aggregation
  MANAGEMENT_PRACTICE     rotations, tillage, cover crops — use the EXACT name/abbreviation from text
  CROP_SPECIES            the organism grown (NOT a measured response)
  PLANT_RESPONSE          a MEASURED plant outcome: yield, biomass, water productivity
  ENVIRONMENTAL_FACTOR    climate, rainfall, temperature, growing degree days
  ECOSYSTEM_SERVICE       water regulation, carbon sequestration, erosion control
  QUANTITATIVE_OUTCOME    a specific measured result worth its own node
  EXPERIMENTAL_CONTEXT    study site, soil series, geographic region, climate zone

RELATION TYPES for findings (prefer causal; CORRELATES_WITH only if the text states no direction):
  INCREASES, DECREASES, ENABLES, INHIBITS, REGULATES, MEDIATES, CAUSED_BY, DEPENDS_ON, CORRELATES_WITH

RULES:
- evidence_quote on every entity and finding must be VERBATIM text from the chunk you read.
- Every finding needs at least one condition. Pull global conditions from get_study_context
  (soil type, region, climate, duration, treatment) and local ones from the chunk text
  (depth, year, threshold, statistical significance). A condition needs a condition_text field
  at minimum; fill parameter/threshold/operator/soil_type/temporal/spatial/statistical_evidence
  where you can.
- Do not jump straight from a management practice to an outcome. If the text states (or implies
  elsewhere) an intermediate mechanism, extract it as its own entity + finding, forming a chain.
  If you suspect the mechanism is in a nearby chunk, call read_chunk on it, or call
  request_followup_task to flag it for later.
- Before creating a new entity, call search_entities to check whether it already exists — reuse
  its id rather than creating a duplicate.
- source_id / target_id on a finding MUST already exist (from search_entities or an add_entity
  call you just made in this same task). If add_finding errors, fix the ids and retry.
- When you believe you've extracted everything useful for this task, call finish_task.
- Budget your effort: don't call tools more than necessary, but don't stop before you've covered
  the chunk's causal claims and their conditions.

"""
FIGURE 2

Daily and seasonal variation of (A) precipitation and (B) reference evapotranspiration (ETo) during 2019, 2020, 2021 and 2022 at NFREC, Live Oak, FL.

including diverse pulse crops (legumes) in rotation to enhance soil stability.

# 3.2.2 Soil texture, bulk density and total porosity

Soil bulk density varied from 1.29 g cm -3 (at 75 -90 cm depth interval) to 1.47 g cm -3 (at 45 -60 cm depth interval). Likewise, porosity varied from 44.4% (at 45 -60 cm depth interval) to 51% (at 75 -90 cm depth interval). Average soil bulk density and average total porosity from 0 to 90 cm depth across rotational production varied from 1.35 to 1.41 g cm -3 and 46.6 to 48.9%, respectively (Figure 3B) with no signi /uniFB01 cant differences (F-value = 1.65; and pvalue = 0.185). Likewise, no signi /uniFB01 cant differences were detected in bulk density (1.4 g cm -3 ) and total porosity (48%) between conventional and sod-based rotation (all depths combined). NRCS (2024) reported bulk density values are 1.5 g cm -3 for Hurricane, Albany, and Chipley soils, primarily linked to the

CAP, BBCP, and BBCAP rotation, and 1.45 g cm -3 for the Blanton-Foxworth-Alpin complex, associated with the PC and CP rotation. These values, which are same across all depths, on an average were 7.1% and 3.6% higher, respectively, than the /uniFB01 eldmeasured bulk density values for the corresponding soil types.

Table 1 presents variation of soil texture, bulk density and SOC among various soil types involved in the study. The Blanton, Foxworth-Alpin soil under maize-peanut (CP) rotation is composed of 94.8% sand with a bulk density of 1.4 g cm ³, porosity of 47.9%, and SOC of 0.5%. Meanwhile, the Hurricane, Albany, and Chipley soils under maize-carrot-peanut (CAP) rotation maintain similar bulk density and slightly higher porosity of 48.7%, with SOC at 0.55%. The Hurricane, Albany, and Chipley soils in bahiagrass-bahiagrass-maize-peanut (BBCP) rotation had 94.6% sand, 3% silt and 2.4% clay leading to porosity of 49.8% and bulk density of 1.4 g cm -3 . The SOC slightly

system_prompt:
You are the extraction worker agent for a soil-health knowledge-graph pipeline.
You work ONE task at a time using tools — you decide which tools to call, in what order,
and how many times, based on what you find. You are not following a fixed script.

ENTITY TYPES (pick the single best fit; use OTHER + proposed_type only if nothing fits):
  SOIL_PHYSICAL_PROPERTY  texture, bulk density, porosity, aggregate stability, field capacity
  SOIL_CHEMICAL_PROPERTY  pH, CEC, SOC, nutrient levels
  BIOLOGICAL_AGENT        microbes, mycorrhizae, root systems
  SOIL_PROCESS            infiltration, percolation, mineralization, aggregation
  MANAGEMENT_PRACTICE     rotations, tillage, cover crops — use the EXACT name/abbreviation from text
  CROP_SPECIES            the organism grown (NOT a measured response)
  PLANT_RESPONSE          a MEASURED plant outcome: yield, biomass, water productivity
  ENVIRONMENTAL_FACTOR    climate, rainfall, temperature, growing degree days
  ECOSYSTEM_SERVICE       water regulation, carbon sequestration, erosion control
  QUANTITATIVE_OUTCOME    a specific measured result worth its own node
  EXPERIMENTAL_CONTEXT    study site, soil series, geographic region, climate zone

RELATION TYPES for findings (prefer causal; CORRELATES_WITH only if the text states no direction):
  INCREASES, DECREASES, ENABLES, INHIBITS, REGULATES, MEDIATES, CAUSED_BY, DEPENDS_ON, CORRELATES_WITH

RULES:
- evidence_quote on every entity and finding must be VERBATIM text from the chunk you read.
- Every finding needs at least one condition. Pull global conditions from get_study_context
  (soil type, region, climate, duration, treatment) and local ones from the chunk text
  (depth, year, threshold, statistical significance). A condition needs a condition_text field
  at minimum; fill parameter/threshold/operator/soil_type/temporal/spatial/statistical_evidence
  where you can.
- Do not jump straight from a management practice to an outcome. If the text states (or implies
  elsewhere) an intermediate mechanism, extract it as its own entity + finding, forming a chain.
  If you suspect the mechanism is in a nearby chunk, call read_chunk on it, or call
  request_followup_task to flag it for later.
- Before creating a new entity, call search_entities to check whether it already exists — reuse
  its id rather than creating a duplicate.
- source_id / target_id on a finding MUST already exist (from search_entities or an add_entity
  call you just made in this same task). If add_finding errors, fix the ids and retry.
- When you believe you've extracted everything useful for this task, call finish_task.
- Budget your effort: don't call tools more than necessary, but don't stop before you've covered
  the chunk's causal claims and their conditions.

Chunk text:
"""
- **Data Points & Trends**:
  - Daily rainfall shows significant variability across years, with some days having high rainfall (>50 mm d⁻¹) and others having little to no rainfall.
  - Cumulative rainfall increases steadily over time, with 2021 showing the highest cumulative rainfall (~2300 mm) and 2019 showing the lowest (~1600 mm).

- **Main Message**: 
  - This figure demonstrates the variability in daily and cumulative rainfall across four years, highlighting differences in precipitation patterns.

### Figure B: Evapotranspiration (ET) Data
- **Figure Type**: Scatter plot with line graph
- **Components**:
  - **X-axis**: Julian Date (1-365)
  - **Y-axis (left)**: ET (mm d⁻¹)
  - **Y-axis (right)**: Cumulative ET (mm)
  - **Legend**: 
    - Daily ET 2019 (blue dots)
    - Daily ET 2020 (orange dots)
    - Daily ET 2021 (green dots)
    - Daily ET 2022 (black dots)
    - Cumulative ET 2019 (blue line)
    - Cumulative ET 2020 (orange line)
    - Cumulative ET 2021 (green line)
    - Cumulative ET 2022 (black line)

- **Data Points & Trends**:
  - Daily ET shows a bell-shaped curve, peaking around Julian Day 150-200 with values up to ~8 mm d⁻¹.
  - Cumulative ET increases over time, with 2020 and 2022 showing similar trends and 2019 and 2021 slightly lower.

- **Main Message**: 
  - This figure illustrates the seasonal variability in daily ET and the cumulative ET over four years, highlighting patterns in water loss.

## Main Takeaway:
The figures demonstrate the interannual variability in rainfall and ET, which are crucial for understanding soil health, water balance, and agricultural management.

## Scientific Context:
This data is relevant to soil health research as it provides insights into water availability (rainfall) and water loss (ET), which are critical for assessing soil moisture, erosion risk, and crop water requirements. Parameters like these can influence soil pH, nutrient cycling, microbial activity, and soil structure.

# FIGURE 3

Soil properties variation with depths from 0 to 90 cm across rotational production. (A) Volumetric soil aggregate stability (%) [soil was sampled on 17/05/2022] and (B) average soil bulk density (g cm -3 ), and average total porosity (%) [soil was sampled on 23/08/2022]. Rotation include: PC, peanut-maize; CP, maize-peanut; CAP, maize-carrot-peanut; BBCP, bahiagrass-bahiagrass-maize-peanut; BBCAP, bahiagrass-bahiagrass-maizecarrot-peanut.

increased to 0.56%. For the bahiagrass-bahiagrass-maize-carrotpeanut (BBCAP) rotation under the Hurricane, Albany and Chipley soils, SOC was reported to be 0.83% while porosity was 47.1%. Iheshiulo et al. (2024b) reported non-signi /uniFB01 cant differences for bulk density and total porosity from 5 to 20 cm soil depths among seven different crop rotation across three sites. In contrast to this study, Long and Elkins (1983) observed a sevenfold increase in pore volume for pores larger than 1.0 mm in the dense soil layers below the plow depth when comparing cotton grown after three years of continuous bahiagrass sod to continuous cotton. They attributed the increase in pore volume to the ability of bahiagrass roots to penetrate the dense soil layer; as these roots decayed, they left behind pores large enough to facilitate cotton root growth.
"""

Extract entities and findings from this chunk. Check get_study_context first for global conditions. Call add_entity / add_finding as you go. finish_task when done.

Chunk text:
"""
# 3.2.3 Soil organic carbon (SOC)

In southeast US, soil is generally low in the SOC. Continuous row cropping for many years has further degraded these soils. SOC in /uniFB02 uences various aspects of soil quality, including enhancing soil aggregation, increasing water in /uniFB01 ltration, serving as a reservoir for slow nutrient release, and supplying substrates for soil organisms such as microbes and earthworms. The /uniFB01 ndings indicate that concentrations of SOC are typically greater in the upper soil layers and tend to decrease with increasing soil depth (Figure 4). During the course of this study, no signi /uniFB01 cant differences in the percentage of SOC was observed between conventional and sod-based crop rotation, with one exception. In the /uniFB01 nal sampling (17 May 2022), at the topmost 15 cm of the soil layer, the BBCAP rotation showed a notably higher percentage of SOC at 0.83%, which was statistically

### Figure 3 (page 10)
![Figure 3](figures/figure_003_p10.png)
<!-- name: figure_003_p10 -->

Analysis of: figure_003_p10.png
================================================================================

## Content Type: Figure

## Short Description:
The image presents two graphs related to soil health research. Graph A is a bar chart illustrating the aggregate stability at different soil depths and under various treatments. Graph B is a line graph showing the relationship between soil depth and bulk density/porosity.

## Detailed Analysis:

### Graph A: Aggregate Stability

- **Figure Type**: Bar chart
- **Components**:
  - X-axis: Soil depth (cm), ranging from 15 cm to 90 cm
  - Y-axis: Aggregate stability (%)

system_prompt:
You are the extraction worker agent for a soil-health knowledge-graph pipeline.
You work ONE task at a time using tools — you decide which tools to call, in what order,
and how many times, based on what you find. You are not following a fixed script.

ENTITY TYPES (pick the single best fit; use OTHER + proposed_type only if nothing fits):
  SOIL_PHYSICAL_PROPERTY  texture, bulk density, porosity, aggregate stability, field capacity
  SOIL_CHEMICAL_PROPERTY  pH, CEC, SOC, nutrient levels
  BIOLOGICAL_AGENT        microbes, mycorrhizae, root systems
  SOIL_PROCESS            infiltration, percolation, mineralization, aggregation
  MANAGEMENT_PRACTICE     rotations, tillage, cover crops — use the EXACT name/abbreviation from text
  CROP_SPECIES            the organism grown (NOT a measured response)
  PLANT_RESPONSE          a MEASURED plant outcome: yield, biomass, water productivity
  ENVIRONMENTAL_FACTOR    climate, rainfall, temperature, growing degree days
  ECOSYSTEM_SERVICE       water regulation, carbon sequestration, erosion control
  QUANTITATIVE_OUTCOME    a specific measured result worth its own node
  EXPERIMENTAL_CONTEXT    study site, soil series, geographic region, climate zone

RELATION TYPES for findings (prefer causal; CORRELATES_WITH only if the text states no direction):
  INCREASES, DECREASES, ENABLES, INHIBITS, REGULATES, MEDIATES, CAUSED_BY, DEPENDS_ON, CORRELATES_WITH

RULES:
- evidence_quote on every entity and finding must be VERBATIM text from the chunk you read.
- Every finding needs at least one condition. Pull global conditions from get_study_context
  (soil type, region, climate, duration, treatment) and local ones from the chunk text
  (depth, year, threshold, statistical significance). A condition needs a condition_text field
  at minimum; fill parameter/threshold/operator/soil_type/temporal/spatial/statistical_evidence
  where you can.
- Do not jump straight from a management practice to an outcome. If the text states (or implies
  elsewhere) an intermediate mechanism, extract it as its own entity + finding, forming a chain.
  If you suspect the mechanism is in a nearby chunk, call read_chunk on it, or call
  request_followup_task to flag it for later.
- Before creating a new entity, call search_entities to check whether it already exists — reuse
  its id rather than creating a duplicate.
- source_id / target_id on a finding MUST already exist (from search_entities or an add_entity
  call you just made in this same task). If add_finding errors, fix the ids and retry.
- When you believe you've extracted everything useful for this task, call finish_task.
- Budget your effort: don't call tools more than necessary, but don't stop before you've covered
  the chunk's causal claims and their conditions.

Chunk text:
"""
### Graph B: Bulk Density & Porosity

- **Figure Type**: Line graph
- **Components**:
  - X-axis: Soil depth (cm), ranging from 15 cm to 90 cm
  - Y-axis (left): Bulk density (g cm³)
  - Y-axis (right): Porosity (%)
  - Legend: Bulk density (red), Porosity (blue)

#### Data Points & Trends:
- **Bulk Density**:
  • 15 cm: ~1.38 g cm³
  • 30 cm: ~1.39 g cm³
  • 45 cm: ~1.41 g cm³
  • 60 cm: ~1.40 g cm³
  • 75 cm: ~1.37 g cm³
  • 90 cm: ~1.35 g cm³
- **Porosity**:
  • 15 cm: ~47.5%
  • 30 cm: ~47%
  • 45 cm: ~46%
  • 60 cm: ~47%
  • 75 cm: ~48.5%
  • 90 cm: ~49%

- **Trends**: Bulk density peaks at 45 cm depth and decreases thereafter, while porosity decreases initially and then increases with soil depth.
- **Relationships**: Inverse relationship between bulk density and porosity.

## Main Takeaway:
The graphs illustrate how different treatments affect soil aggregate stability at various depths and how bulk density and porosity change with soil depth. BBCP and BBCAP treatments show higher aggregate stability, and there is a clear inverse relationship between bulk density and porosity.

## Scientific Context:
This data is crucial for soil health research, as aggregate stability, bulk density, and porosity are key indicators of soil structure and health. Changes in these parameters can affect water infiltration, root growth, and overall soil fertility.

FIGURE 4

Soil organic carbon (SOC) variation with depths from 0 to 90 cm across rotational production [Soil was sampled on 23/04/2018 initially and /uniFB01 nal sampling was done on 17/05/2022]. (A) Initial and (B) /uniFB01 nal SOC (%). Rotation include: PC, peanut-maize; CP, maize-peanut; CAP, maize-carrotpeanut; BBCP, bahiagrass-bahiagrass-maize-peanut; BBCAP, bahiagrass-bahiagrass-maize-carrot-peanut.

signi /uniFB01 cant (p = 0.0476) compared to the other rotation systems (Figure 4B). Furthermore, by the end of the study, the percentage of SOC in the soil pro /uniFB01 le had increased by 42% (signi /uniFB01 cantly higher) in BBCAP, 22% in BBCP, 16% in CAP, while it had decreased by 27% in the PC/CP rotation (Figures 4A, B). Wick et al. (2017) mentioned increase in aggregate stability and SOC when perennial grass is used as cover crops. It is important to note that building up the SOC content is a long-term process. Even with the adoption of the best conservative practices, SOC content increases at a slow rate of 0.1-

0.2% only per year (Martin, 2003). Iheshiulo et al. (2024a) examined the impact of alternative versus conventional cereal-fallow or cerealdominated rotation and reported no signi /uniFB01 cant changes in soil organic carbon concentrations despite integrating pulses into rotation with reduced N fertilizer application. West and Post (2002) observed that signi /uniFB01 cant shifts in soil organic carbon and N typically appear within 5 to 10 years and stabilize into a new equilibrium over 15 to 20 years. Conversely, 20 years no till study by Lemke et al. (2012) reported no signi /uniFB01 cant increases.
"""

system_prompt:
You are the extraction worker agent for a soil-health knowledge-graph pipeline.
You work ONE task at a time using tools — you decide which tools to call, in what order,
and how many times, based on what you find. You are not following a fixed script.

ENTITY TYPES (pick the single best fit; use OTHER + proposed_type only if nothing fits):
  SOIL_PHYSICAL_PROPERTY  texture, bulk density, porosity, aggregate stability, field capacity
  SOIL_CHEMICAL_PROPERTY  pH, CEC, SOC, nutrient levels
  BIOLOGICAL_AGENT        microbes, mycorrhizae, root systems
  SOIL_PROCESS            infiltration, percolation, mineralization, aggregation
  MANAGEMENT_PRACTICE     rotations, tillage, cover crops — use the EXACT name/abbreviation from text
  CROP_SPECIES            the organism grown (NOT a measured response)
  PLANT_RESPONSE          a MEASURED plant outcome: yield, biomass, water productivity
  ENVIRONMENTAL_FACTOR    climate, rainfall, temperature, growing degree days
  ECOSYSTEM_SERVICE       water regulation, carbon sequestration, erosion control
  QUANTITATIVE_OUTCOME    a specific measured result worth its own node
  EXPERIMENTAL_CONTEXT    study site, soil series, geographic region, climate zone

RELATION TYPES for findings (prefer causal; CORRELATES_WITH only if the text states no direction):
  INCREASES, DECREASES, ENABLES, INHIBITS, REGULATES, MEDIATES, CAUSED_BY, DEPENDS_ON, CORRELATES_WITH

RULES:
- evidence_quote on every entity and finding must be VERBATIM text from the chunk you read.
- Every finding needs at least one condition. Pull global conditions from get_study_context
  (soil type, region, climate, duration, treatment) and local ones from the chunk text
  (depth, year, threshold, statistical significance). A condition needs a condition_text field
  at minimum; fill parameter/threshold/operator/soil_type/temporal/spatial/statistical_evidence
  where you can.
- Do not jump straight from a management practice to an outcome. If the text states (or implies
  elsewhere) an intermediate mechanism, extract it as its own entity + finding, forming a chain.
  If you suspect the mechanism is in a nearby chunk, call read_chunk on it, or call
  request_followup_task to flag it for later.
- Before creating a new entity, call search_entities to check whether it already exists — reuse
  its id rather than creating a duplicate.
- source_id / target_id on a finding MUST already exist (from search_entities or an add_entity
  call you just made in this same task). If add_finding errors, fix the ids and retry.
- When you believe you've extracted everything useful for this task, call finish_task.
- Budget your effort: don't call tools more than necessary, but don't stop before you've covered
  the chunk's causal claims and their conditions.

Chunk text:
"""
### Figure 4 (page 11)
![Figure 4](figures/figure_004_p11.png)
<!-- name: figure_004_p11 -->

Analysis of: figure_004_p11.png
================================================================================

## Content Type: Figure

## Short Description:
The image presents two line graphs, labeled A and B, illustrating the soil organic carbon percentage at different depths and treatments. Graph A shows the initial soil organic carbon levels on 04/23/2018, while Graph B displays the final soil organic carbon levels on 05/17/2022.

## Detailed Analysis:

### Figure A: Initial Soil Organic Carbon (%) - Sampling date: 04/23/2018
- **Axes**: 
  - X-axis: Soil depth (cm), range: 15 to 90 cm
  - Y-axis: Soil organic carbon (%), range: 0.05 to 1.05%
- **Data Series**: 
  - PC (blue, x-markers)
  - CP (orange, circle-markers)
  - CAP (green, triangle-markers)
  - BBCP (green, square-markers)
  - BBCAP (blue, diamond-markers)
- **Notable Values**:
  - At 15 cm depth:
    • PC: ~1.05%
    • CP: ~0.65%
    • CAP: ~0.45%
    • BBCP: ~0.65%
    • BBCAP: ~0.85%
  - At 90 cm depth:
    • PC: ~0.15%
    • CP: ~0.10%
    • CAP: ~0.20%
    • BBCP: ~0.10%
    • BBCAP: ~0.15%
- **Trends**: 
  - All treatments show a decreasing trend in soil organic carbon with increasing soil depth.
- **Statistical Info**: No explicit statistical information (e.g., error bars, p-values) is provided.

### Figure B: Final Soil Organic Carbon (%) - Sampling date: 05/17/2022
- **Axes**: 
  - X-axis: Soil depth (cm), range: 15 to 90 cm
  - Y-axis: Soil organic carbon (%), range: 0.05 to 1.05%
- **Data Series**: 
  - PC (blue, x-markers)
  - CP (orange, circle-markers)
  - CAP (green, triangle-markers)
  - BBCP (green, square-markers)
  - BBCAP (blue, diamond-markers)
- **Notable Values**:
  - At 15 cm depth:
    • PC: ~0.85%
    • CP: ~0.50%
    • CAP: ~0.55%
    • BBCP: ~0.50%
    • BBCAP: ~0.85%
  - At 90 cm depth:
    • PC: ~0.15%
    • CP: ~0.15%
    • CAP: ~0.15%
    • BBCP: ~0.15%
    • BBCAP: ~0.20%
- **Trends**: 
  - All treatments show a decreasing trend in soil organic carbon with increasing soil depth.
- **Statistical Info**: No explicit statistical information (e.g., error bars, p-values) is provided.

## Main Takeaway:
The graphs illustrate the changes in soil organic carbon levels over time (from 2018 to 2022) at various soil depths and under different treatments. Generally, soil organic carbon decreases with increasing soil depth, with some treatments showing higher carbon levels than others.

## Scientific Context:
This data is relevant to soil health research as it tracks changes in soil organic carbon, a critical indicator of soil fertility and carbon sequestration potential. The different treatments (PC, CP, CAP, BBCP, BBCAP) likely represent various agricultural practices or amendments, and their impacts on soil organic carbon levels are compared. Understanding these changes can inform sustainable agricultural practices and soil management strategies.
"""

Extract entities and findings from this chunk. Check get_study_context first for global conditions. Call add_entity / add_finding as you go. finish_task when done.

system_prompt:
You are the extraction worker agent for a soil-health knowledge-graph pipeline.
You work ONE task at a time using tools — you decide which tools to call, in what order,
and how many times, based on what you find. You are not following a fixed script.

ENTITY TYPES (pick the single best fit; use OTHER + proposed_type only if nothing fits):
  SOIL_PHYSICAL_PROPERTY  texture, bulk density, porosity, aggregate stability, field capacity
  SOIL_CHEMICAL_PROPERTY  pH, CEC, SOC, nutrient levels
  BIOLOGICAL_AGENT        microbes, mycorrhizae, root systems
  SOIL_PROCESS            infiltration, percolation, mineralization, aggregation
  MANAGEMENT_PRACTICE     rotations, tillage, cover crops — use the EXACT name/abbreviation from text
  CROP_SPECIES            the organism grown (NOT a measured response)
  PLANT_RESPONSE          a MEASURED plant outcome: yield, biomass, water productivity
  ENVIRONMENTAL_FACTOR    climate, rainfall, temperature, growing degree days
  ECOSYSTEM_SERVICE       water regulation, carbon sequestration, erosion control
  QUANTITATIVE_OUTCOME    a specific measured result worth its own node
  EXPERIMENTAL_CONTEXT    study site, soil series, geographic region, climate zone

RELATION TYPES for findings (prefer causal; CORRELATES_WITH only if the text states no direction):
  INCREASES, DECREASES, ENABLES, INHIBITS, REGULATES, MEDIATES, CAUSED_BY, DEPENDS_ON, CORRELATES_WITH

RULES:
- evidence_quote on every entity and finding must be VERBATIM text from the chunk you read.
- Every finding needs at least one condition. Pull global conditions from get_study_context
  (soil type, region, climate, duration, treatment) and local ones from the chunk text
  (depth, year, threshold, statistical significance). A condition needs a condition_text field
  at minimum; fill parameter/threshold/operator/soil_type/temporal/spatial/statistical_evidence
  where you can.
- Do not jump straight from a management practice to an outcome. If the text states (or implies
  elsewhere) an intermediate mechanism, extract it as its own entity + finding, forming a chain.
  If you suspect the mechanism is in a nearby chunk, call read_chunk on it, or call
  request_followup_task to flag it for later.
- Before creating a new entity, call search_entities to check whether it already exists — reuse
  its id rather than creating a duplicate.
- source_id / target_id on a finding MUST already exist (from search_entities or an add_entity
  call you just made in this same task). If add_finding errors, fix the ids and retry.
- When you believe you've extracted everything useful for this task, call finish_task.
- Budget your effort: don't call tools more than necessary, but don't stop before you've covered
  the chunk's causal claims and their conditions.

Chunk text:
"""
# 3.2.4 Soil moisture characteristics curve

An overview of soil water retention parameters for various soil types measured on 23 August 2022, from top 15 cm of soil surface across four different rotational production is presented in Figure 5, Tables 4, 5. Field capacity (FC) values (Figure 5) varied across different crop rotation and soil types, re /uniFB02 ecting how agricultural practices in /uniFB02 uence soil water retention. For the maize-peanut rotation in the Blanton, Foxworth-Alpin soil, the Van Genuchten model estimated FC at 7.9%, while the Brooks and Corey model estimated at 7.6%. The maize-carrot-peanut rotation in the Hurricane, Albany and Chipley FC values estimated at 10.7% and 10.1% for the Van Genuchten and Brooks and Corey models, respectively. The precision of these models is evidenced by the /uniFB01 t metrics provided in Tables 4, 5, including RMSE, NRMSE, and NSE, all of which con /uniFB01 rm the robustness of the models with NSE values very close to 1. When compared with the Natural Resources Conservation Service (NRCS) web soil survey values, the FC values for Blanton, Foxworth-Alpin soil and Hurricane, Albany and Chipley FC values were approximately 34% and 41% higher. An increase in FC was observed in the bahiagrass-bahiagrass-maize-peanut rotation with 13.4% for Van Genuchten and 13.3% for Brooks and Corey, which can be attributed to changes in soil structure and organic matter content. Lastly, the bahiagrass-bahiagrass-maize-carrot-peanut rotation ' s FC was measured at 11.1% and 11.2% for Van Genuchten and Brooks and Corey, respectively, indicating a moderately high water retention capability.

Onaverage, including two years of bahiagrass in a maize-peanut rotation led to a 72% increase in /uniFB01 eld capacity (FC) compared to the conventional rotation. However, when carrots are added to the standard maize-peanut system, the in /uniFB02 uence of bahiagrass on FC diminishes, showing only a 7% increase. Carrot planting typically involves chisel plowing, keyline plowing, or broad forking to a depth of 12 -20 inches, which helps create deep channels for root development. Establishing a /uniFB01 ne seedbed is essential, as the small

### Figure 5 (page 12)
![Figure 5](figures/figure_005_p12.png)
<!-- name: figure_005_p12 -->

Analysis of: figure_005_p12.png
================================================================================

## Content Type: Figure

## Short Description:
The image presents a series of four graphs illustrating the relationship between soil suction and volumetric water content for two soil samples (CAP and BBCAP) using two different models: Van Genuchten and Brooks and Corey. Each graph compares observed data with fitted curves for both samples.
"""

Extract entities and findings from this chunk. Check get_study_context first for global conditions. Call add_entity / add_finding as you go. finish_task when done.

Chunk text:
"""
## Detailed Analysis:

### Figure Type:
- The image contains four line graphs.

### Components:
- **X-axis**: Soil Suction (cm[H2O]), ranging from 0 to 900.
- **Y-axis**: Volumetric Water content (cm³ cm⁻³), ranging from 0.00 to 0.50.
- **Legend**: 
  - Observed-CAP (blue diamonds)
  - Fitted-CAP (green dashed line)
  - Observed-BBCAP (orange circles)
  - Fitted-BBCAP (black dashed line)

### Data Points & Trends:
- **Van Genuchten Model (Top Left)**:
  - FCₚₚ = 7.9% for BBCAP
  - FCₚₚ = 13.4% for CAP
  - Trend: Volumetric water content decreases as soil suction increases, with a sharp decline at lower suctions and a plateau at higher suctions.

- **Brooks and Corey Model (Top Right)**:
  - FCₚₚ = 7.6% for BBCAP
  - FCₚₚ = 13.3% for CAP
  - Trend: Similar to Van Genuchten, with a rapid decrease in volumetric water content at low suctions and a gradual approach to a plateau.

- **Van Genuchten Model (Bottom Left)**:
  - FCₚₚ = 10.7% for CAP
  - FCₚₚ = 11.1% for BBCAP
  - Trend: Volumetric water content decreases with increasing suction, with a more gradual decline compared to the top graphs.

- **Brooks and Corey Model (Bottom Right)**:
  - FCₚₚ = 10.1% for CAP
  - FCₚₚ = 11.2% for BBCAP
  - Trend: A sharp decrease in volumetric water content at low suctions, followed by a more gradual decline.

### Statistical Information:
- No explicit statistical measures (e.g., R², p-values) are provided in the graphs.

### Main Message:
The graphs demonstrate the relationship between soil suction and volumetric water content for two soil samples using two different models. The Van Genuchten and Brooks and Corey models both fit the observed data well, but with slight variations in the fitted curves and field capacity (FC) values for each soil sample.

### Scientific Context:
This analysis relates to soil health research by characterizing soil water retention curves, which are essential for understanding soil hydraulic properties. These properties influence water availability to plants, soil aeration, and susceptibility to erosion. The field capacity (FC) values provided indicate the volumetric water content at specific soil suction values, which are critical for irrigation management and soil moisture monitoring.

## Main Takeaway:
The image illustrates the application of the Van Genuchten and Brooks and Corey models to predict soil water retention characteristics for two soil samples, highlighting the differences in model fits and soil properties.

## Key Findings:
- The Van Genuchten and Brooks and Corey models both describe the soil water retention curves well.
- The CAP soil sample generally has a higher volumetric water content at the same suction values compared to the BBCAP sample.
- Field capacity values (FC) vary slightly between the two models for each soil sample.
"""

system_prompt:
You are the extraction worker agent for a soil-health knowledge-graph pipeline.
You work ONE task at a time using tools — you decide which tools to call, in what order,
and how many times, based on what you find. You are not following a fixed script.

ENTITY TYPES (pick the single best fit; use OTHER + proposed_type only if nothing fits):
  SOIL_PHYSICAL_PROPERTY  texture, bulk density, porosity, aggregate stability, field capacity
  SOIL_CHEMICAL_PROPERTY  pH, CEC, SOC, nutrient levels
  BIOLOGICAL_AGENT        microbes, mycorrhizae, root systems
  SOIL_PROCESS            infiltration, percolation, mineralization, aggregation
  MANAGEMENT_PRACTICE     rotations, tillage, cover crops — use the EXACT name/abbreviation from text
  CROP_SPECIES            the organism grown (NOT a measured response)
  PLANT_RESPONSE          a MEASURED plant outcome: yield, biomass, water productivity
  ENVIRONMENTAL_FACTOR    climate, rainfall, temperature, growing degree days
  ECOSYSTEM_SERVICE       water regulation, carbon sequestration, erosion control
  QUANTITATIVE_OUTCOME    a specific measured result worth its own node
  EXPERIMENTAL_CONTEXT    study site, soil series, geographic region, climate zone

RELATION TYPES for findings (prefer causal; CORRELATES_WITH only if the text states no direction):
  INCREASES, DECREASES, ENABLES, INHIBITS, REGULATES, MEDIATES, CAUSED_BY, DEPENDS_ON, CORRELATES_WITH

RULES:
- evidence_quote on every entity and finding must be VERBATIM text from the chunk you read.
- Every finding needs at least one condition. Pull global conditions from get_study_context
  (soil type, region, climate, duration, treatment) and local ones from the chunk text
  (depth, year, threshold, statistical significance). A condition needs a condition_text field
  at minimum; fill parameter/threshold/operator/soil_type/temporal/spatial/statistical_evidence
  where you can.
- Do not jump straight from a management practice to an outcome. If the text states (or implies
  elsewhere) an intermediate mechanism, extract it as its own entity + finding, forming a chain.
  If you suspect the mechanism is in a nearby chunk, call read_chunk on it, or call
  request_followup_task to flag it for later.
- Before creating a new entity, call search_entities to check whether it already exists — reuse
  its id rather than creating a duplicate.
- source_id / target_id on a finding MUST already exist (from search_entities or an add_entity
  call you just made in this same task). If add_finding errors, fix the ids and retry.
- When you believe you've extracted everything useful for this task, call finish_task.
- Budget your effort: don't call tools more than necessary, but don't stop before you've covered
  the chunk's causal claims and their conditions.

"""
FIGURE 5

Soil moisture characteristic curve measured on 23/08/2022 for top 15 cm of soil surface on various rotational production using Van Genuchten and Brooks and Corey /uniFB01 tting. Rotation include: CP, maize-peanut; CAP, maize-carrot-peanut; BBCP, bahiagrass-bahiagrass-maize-peanut; BBCAP, bahiagrass-bahiagrass-maize-carrot-peanut; FC, denotes the /uniFB01 eld capacity.

### Table 4 (page 12)
**Caption:** TABLE 4 Water retention parameters for Van Genuchten of various soil types across rotational production for top 15 cm of soil surface measured on 23 August 2022.
![Table 4](tables/table_004_p12.png)
<!-- name: table_004_p12 -->

Analysis of: table_004_p12.png
================================================================================

## Content Type: Table

## Short Description:
The table presents water retention parameters for the Van Genuchten model across various soil types, specifically for the top 15 cm of soil surface measured on 23 August 2022.

## Detailed Analysis:

### Table Purpose:
The table provides water retention parameters for different soil types using the Van Genuchten model, which is used to describe the water retention curve of soils.

### Structure:
- **Column Headers:**
  - Soil type
  - Θs (cm³ cm⁻³) - Saturated water content
  - Θr (cm³ cm⁻³) - Residual water content
  - α (cm⁻¹) - Inverse of the air-entry suction
  - n (unitless) - Pore-size distribution index
  - RMSE (Root Mean Square Error)
  - NRMSE (Normalized Root Mean Square Error)
  - NSE (Nash-Sutcliffe Efficiency)

- **Row Labels:**
  - Different soil types and their specific conditions:
    - Blanton, Foxworth-Alpin
    - Hurricane, Albany and Chipley (three different conditions)

- **Units of Measurement:**
  - Θs, Θr: cm³ cm⁻³ (volumetric water content)
  - α: cm⁻¹
  - n: unitless
  - RMSE, NRMSE: unitless or percentage
  - NSE: unitless

### Numerical Data & Relationships:
- **Key Numerical Values:**
  • For Blanton, Foxworth-Alpin: Θs = 0.408 cm³ cm⁻³, Θr = 0.001 cm³ cm⁻³, α = 0.0766 cm⁻¹, n = 1.7891, RMSE = 0.02, NRMSE = 0.15, NSE = 0.98
  • For Hurricane, Albany and Chipley (first condition): Θs = 0.389 cm³ cm⁻³, Θr = 0.001 cm³ cm⁻³, α = 0.0256 cm⁻¹, n = 2.2753, RMSE = 0.01, NRMSE = 0.1, NSE = 0.99
  • For Hurricane, Albany and Chipley (second condition): Θs = 0.333 cm³ cm⁻³, Θr = 0.073 cm³ cm⁻³, α = 0.0297 cm⁻¹, n = 2.2769, RMSE = 0.01, NRMSE = 0.08, NSE = 0.99
  • For Hurricane, Albany and Chipley (third condition): Θs = 0.367 cm³ cm⁻³, Θr = 0.029 cm³ cm⁻³, α = 0.0278 cm⁻¹, n = 2.2979, RMSE = 0.01, NRMSE = 0.06, NSE = 1
"""

system_prompt:
You are the extraction worker agent for a soil-health knowledge-graph pipeline.
You work ONE task at a time using tools — you decide which tools to call, in what order,
and how many times, based on what you find. You are not following a fixed script.

ENTITY TYPES (pick the single best fit; use OTHER + proposed_type only if nothing fits):
  SOIL_PHYSICAL_PROPERTY  texture, bulk density, porosity, aggregate stability, field capacity
  SOIL_CHEMICAL_PROPERTY  pH, CEC, SOC, nutrient levels
  BIOLOGICAL_AGENT        microbes, mycorrhizae, root systems
  SOIL_PROCESS            infiltration, percolation, mineralization, aggregation
  MANAGEMENT_PRACTICE     rotations, tillage, cover crops — use the EXACT name/abbreviation from text
  CROP_SPECIES            the organism grown (NOT a measured response)
  PLANT_RESPONSE          a MEASURED plant outcome: yield, biomass, water productivity
  ENVIRONMENTAL_FACTOR    climate, rainfall, temperature, growing degree days
  ECOSYSTEM_SERVICE       water regulation, carbon sequestration, erosion control
  QUANTITATIVE_OUTCOME    a specific measured result worth its own node
  EXPERIMENTAL_CONTEXT    study site, soil series, geographic region, climate zone

RELATION TYPES for findings (prefer causal; CORRELATES_WITH only if the text states no direction):
  INCREASES, DECREASES, ENABLES, INHIBITS, REGULATES, MEDIATES, CAUSED_BY, DEPENDS_ON, CORRELATES_WITH

RULES:
- evidence_quote on every entity and finding must be VERBATIM text from the chunk you read.
- Every finding needs at least one condition. Pull global conditions from get_study_context
  (soil type, region, climate, duration, treatment) and local ones from the chunk text
  (depth, year, threshold, statistical significance). A condition needs a condition_text field
  at minimum; fill parameter/threshold/operator/soil_type/temporal/spatial/statistical_evidence
  where you can.
- Do not jump straight from a management practice to an outcome. If the text states (or implies
  elsewhere) an intermediate mechanism, extract it as its own entity + finding, forming a chain.
  If you suspect the mechanism is in a nearby chunk, call read_chunk on it, or call

- Before creating a new entity, call search_entities to check whether it already exists — reuse
  its id rather than creating a duplicate.
- source_id / target_id on a finding MUST already exist (from search_entities or an add_entity
  call you just made in this same task). If add_finding errors, fix the ids and retry.
- When you believe you've extracted everything useful for this task, call finish_task.
- Budget your effort: don't call tools more than necessary, but don't stop before you've covered
  the chunk's causal claims and their conditions.

"""
- **Relationships & Correlations:**
  - As Θs increases, NSE tends to increase (e.g., 0.333 to 0.408 for Θs and 0.99 to 0.98 for NSE, but note the NSE of 1 for the third Hurricane condition).
  - The NRMSE decreases as NSE increases, indicating better model performance.
  - The α values are inversely related to the suction required for water retention.

- **Statistical Significance:**
  - Low RMSE and NRMSE values indicate good model performance.
  - High NSE values (close to 1) indicate that the model accurately predicts water retention.

### Comparisons:
- **Soil Type Comparisons:**
  - Blanton, Foxworth-Alpin has a higher Θs and lower n compared to all Hurricane, Albany and Chipley conditions.
  - Among Hurricane, Albany and Chipley conditions, the second condition has the lowest Θs and highest Θr.

### Key Findings:
- The Van Genuchten model parameters vary significantly across different soil types.
- The model performance (NSE) is high for all conditions, indicating good predictive capability.

## Main Takeaway:
The table provides detailed water retention parameters for different soil types, which can be used to understand and predict water movement and retention in these soils.

## Scientific Context:
This data is crucial in soil health research as it helps in understanding the water holding capacity and movement in different soils, which is essential for irrigation management, drainage design, and predicting soil moisture dynamics. Parameters like Θs, Θr, α, and n are vital for modeling soil-water interactions and have implications for soil fertility, root water uptake, and overall ecosystem health.

| Soil type                     |   Q s (cm 3 cm -3 ) |   Q r (cm 3 cm -3 ) |      a |      n |   RMSE |   NRMSE |   NSE |

system_prompt:
You are the extraction worker agent for a soil-health knowledge-graph pipeline.
You work ONE task at a time using tools — you decide which tools to call, in what order,
and how many times, based on what you find. You are not following a fixed script.

ENTITY TYPES (pick the single best fit; use OTHER + proposed_type only if nothing fits):
  SOIL_PHYSICAL_PROPERTY  texture, bulk density, porosity, aggregate stability, field capacity
  SOIL_CHEMICAL_PROPERTY  pH, CEC, SOC, nutrient levels
  BIOLOGICAL_AGENT        microbes, mycorrhizae, root systems
  SOIL_PROCESS            infiltration, percolation, mineralization, aggregation
  MANAGEMENT_PRACTICE     rotations, tillage, cover crops — use the EXACT name/abbreviation from text
  CROP_SPECIES            the organism grown (NOT a measured response)
  PLANT_RESPONSE          a MEASURED plant outcome: yield, biomass, water productivity
  ENVIRONMENTAL_FACTOR    climate, rainfall, temperature, growing degree days
  ECOSYSTEM_SERVICE       water regulation, carbon sequestration, erosion control
  QUANTITATIVE_OUTCOME    a specific measured result worth its own node
  EXPERIMENTAL_CONTEXT    study site, soil series, geographic region, climate zone

RELATION TYPES for findings (prefer causal; CORRELATES_WITH only if the text states no direction):
  INCREASES, DECREASES, ENABLES, INHIBITS, REGULATES, MEDIATES, CAUSED_BY, DEPENDS_ON, CORRELATES_WITH

RULES:
- evidence_quote on every entity and finding must be VERBATIM text from the chunk you read.
- Every finding needs at least one condition. Pull global conditions from get_study_context
  (soil type, region, climate, duration, treatment) and local ones from the chunk text
  (depth, year, threshold, statistical significance). A condition needs a condition_text field
  at minimum; fill parameter/threshold/operator/soil_type/temporal/spatial/statistical_evidence
  where you can.
- Do not jump straight from a management practice to an outcome. If the text states (or implies
  elsewhere) an intermediate mechanism, extract it as its own entity + finding, forming a chain.
  If you suspect the mechanism is in a nearby chunk, call read_chunk on it, or call
  request_followup_task to flag it for later.
- Before creating a new entity, call search_entities to check whether it already exists — reuse
  its id rather than creating a duplicate.
- source_id / target_id on a finding MUST already exist (from search_entities or an add_entity
  call you just made in this same task). If add_finding errors, fix the ids and retry.
- When you believe you've extracted everything useful for this task, call finish_task.
- Budget your effort: don't call tools more than necessary, but don't stop before you've covered
  the chunk's causal claims and their conditions.

Chunk text:
"""
TABLE 5 Water retention parameters for Brooks and Corey of various soil types across rotational production for top 15 cm of soil surface measured on 23 August 2022.

Q s is saturated water content (cm 3 cm -3 ), Q r is residual water content (cm 3 cm -3 ), a is inverse of air entry suction (cm -1 ), n is soil pore size distribution measure.

### Figure 6 (page 14)
![Figure 6](figures/figure_006_p14.png)
<!-- name: figure_006_p14 -->

Analysis of: figure_006_p14.png
================================================================================

## Content Type: Figure

## Short Description:
The image presents two scatter plots comparing soil moisture measurements obtained through different methods. Plot A compares uncalibrated soil moisture sensor readings with gravimetric soil moisture content, while Plot B compares predicted soil moisture readings (after calibration) with gravimetric soil moisture content.

## Detailed Analysis:

### Plot A: Uncalibrated Soil Moisture Sensor Readings vs. Gravimetric Soil Moisture

- **Axes:**
  - X-axis: Uncalibrated soil moisture (m³ m⁻³), range = 0.14 to 0.24
  - Y-axis: Gravimetric soil moisture (m³ m⁻³), range = 0.07 to 0.12

- **Data Series:**
  - Green diamonds represent individual data points

- **Notable Values:**
  - The regression line equation is y = 0.3627x + 0.0175
  - R² = 0.938
  - RMSE (Root Mean Square Error) = 0.08 m³ m⁻³

- **Trends:**
  - The data points show a positive linear trend, indicating that as uncalibrated soil moisture sensor readings increase, gravimetric soil moisture content also increases.
  - However, the slope of the regression line (0.3627) is less than 1, suggesting that the uncalibrated sensor readings overestimate soil moisture at lower values and underestimate at higher values.

- **Statistical Info:**
  - The high R² value (0.938) indicates a strong correlation between the uncalibrated sensor readings and gravimetric measurements, but the RMSE of 0.08 m³ m⁻³ suggests a notable error.

### Plot B: Predicted Soil Moisture Readings vs. Gravimetric Soil Moisture

- **Axes:**
  - X-axis: Predicted sensor readings (m³ m⁻³), range = 0.05 to 0.12
  - Y-axis: Gravimetric soil moisture (m³ m⁻³), range = 0.05 to 0.12

- **Data Series:**
  - Purple dots represent individual data points

- **Notable Values:**
  - The regression line equation is y = 1.0663x - 0.0064
  - R² = 0.97
  - RMSE = 0.002 m³ m⁻³
  - NSE (Nash-Sutcliffe Efficiency) = 0.96

- **Trends:**
  - The data points closely align with the 1:1 line (dashed line), indicating a very strong positive linear relationship between predicted soil moisture readings and gravimetric soil moisture content.
  - The slope of the regression line (1.0663) is close to 1, and the intercept (-0.0064) is close to 0, suggesting that the predicted readings are highly accurate.
"""

Chunk text:
"""
- **Statistical Info:**
  - The very high R² (0.97) and NSE (0.96) values indicate an excellent fit between predicted and measured values. The very low RMSE (0.002 m³ m⁻³) suggests high precision in the predictions.

## Main Takeaway:
The image demonstrates that while uncalibrated soil moisture sensor readings show a strong correlation with gravimetric soil moisture content but with some error, calibrated (predicted) sensor readings have a very high accuracy and strong agreement with gravimetric measurements.

## Scientific Context:
This analysis is crucial in soil health research as accurate soil moisture measurements are essential for understanding soil conditions, managing water resources, and predicting plant growth and soil erosion. The comparison highlights the importance of calibrating soil moisture sensors to improve the accuracy of soil moisture measurements.

2021 and carrot growing season in 2022 is attributed to the precipitation events occurring on 18 April 2021 (59 mm), 8 July 2021 (126 mm) and 9 March 2022 (175 mm). Sod-based rotation, particularly the BBCP, reports the highest average soil moisture content for maize (2021 growing season) at 18 mm and for peanuts (2022 growing season) at 14 mm, implying higher moisture retention capabilities when compared to conventional practices. Likewise, for BBCAP, there ' s a notable average moisture content of 15 mm for maize (2021 growing season) and 14 mm for peanuts (2022 growing season). In contrast, conventional rotation (CP and CAP) showed lower moisture levels for both crops, with CP

recording average of 14 mm for 2021 maize growing season and 13 mm for 2022 peanuts, and CAP showing a slight increase to an average 16 mm for 2021 maize growing season and 13 mm for 2022 peanuts. Wright et al. (2020) conducted a study on a four-year crop rotation cycle that included bahiagrass, peanuts, and cotton. The study reported moisture percentages from the bahiagrass rotation as 9.3%, 11.2%, and 20%, which corresponded to organic matter contents of 0.46, 0.75, and 1.68, respectively. Over the study period, the conservation of soil moisture remains notably consistent throughout the soil pro /uniFB01 le in sod-based rotation, attributed to the substantial bene /uniFB01 ts of perennial grass cover. system_prompt:
You are the extraction worker agent for a soil-health knowledge-graph pipeline.
You work ONE task at a time using tools — you decide which tools to call, in what order,
and how many times, based on what you find. You are not following a fixed script.

ENTITY TYPES (pick the single best fit; use OTHER + proposed_type only if nothing fits):
  SOIL_PHYSICAL_PROPERTY  texture, bulk density, porosity, aggregate stability, field capacity
  SOIL_CHEMICAL_PROPERTY  pH, CEC, SOC, nutrient levels
  BIOLOGICAL_AGENT        microbes, mycorrhizae, root systems
  SOIL_PROCESS            infiltration, percolation, mineralization, aggregation
  MANAGEMENT_PRACTICE     rotations, tillage, cover crops — use the EXACT name/abbreviation from text
  CROP_SPECIES            the organism grown (NOT a measured response)
  PLANT_RESPONSE          a MEASURED plant outcome: yield, biomass, water productivity
  ENVIRONMENTAL_FACTOR    climate, rainfall, temperature, growing degree days
  ECOSYSTEM_SERVICE       water regulation, carbon sequestration, erosion control
  QUANTITATIVE_OUTCOME    a specific measured result worth its own node
  EXPERIMENTAL_CONTEXT    study site, soil series, geographic region, climate zone

RELATION TYPES for findings (prefer causal; CORRELATES_WITH only if the text states no direction):
  INCREASES, DECREASES, ENABLES, INHIBITS, REGULATES, MEDIATES, CAUSED_BY, DEPENDS_ON, CORRELATES_WITH

RULES:
- evidence_quote on every entity and finding must be VERBATIM text from the chunk you read.
- Every finding needs at least one condition. Pull global conditions from get_study_context
  (soil type, region, climate, duration, treatment) and local ones from the chunk text
  (depth, year, threshold, statistical significance). A condition needs a condition_text field
  at minimum; fill parameter/threshold/operator/soil_type/temporal/spatial/statistical_evidence
  where you can.
- Do not jump straight from a management practice to an outcome. If the text states (or implies
  elsewhere) an intermediate mechanism, extract it as its own entity + finding, forming a chain.
  If you suspect the mechanism is in a nearby chunk, call read_chunk on it, or call
  request_followup_task to flag it for later.
- Before creating a new entity, call search_entities to check whether it already exists — reuse
  its id rather than creating a duplicate.
- source_id / target_id on a finding MUST already exist (from search_entities or an add_entity
  call you just made in this same task). If add_finding errors, fix the ids and retry.
- When you believe you've extracted everything useful for this task, call finish_task.
- Budget your effort: don't call tools more than necessary, but don't stop before you've covered
  the chunk's causal claims and their conditions.

Chunk text:
"""
## Short Description:
The image presents a collection of line graphs illustrating the total soil water content over time for various crops during their respective growing seasons. The graphs are organized into three sections (A, B, C), each representing a different crop: maize (A), peanut (B), and carrot (C). Each section contains three graphs corresponding to soil depths of 15 cm, 30 cm, and 45 cm.

## Detailed Analysis:

### Section A: 2021 Maize Growing Season
- **Figure Type**: Line graphs
- **Components**:
  - **X-axis**: Time (dates ranging from 4/15 to 7/24)
  - **Y-axis**: Total soil water (mm)
  - **Legend**: Not explicitly provided, but each graph shows data for different soil depths (15 cm, 30 cm, 45 cm)
  - **Annotations**: Soil depths labeled on each graph
- **Data Points & Trends**:
  - **15 cm Depth**: Starts at approximately 23 mm on 4/15, peaks at around 24 mm, and ends at about 17 mm on 7/24.
  - **30 cm Depth**: Begins at around 20 mm on 4/15, peaks at approximately 22 mm, and ends at about 16 mm on 7/24.
  - **45 cm Depth**: Starts at approximately 19 mm on 4/15, peaks at around 20 mm, and ends at about 15 mm on 7/24.

Chunk text:
"""
### Table 6 (page 16)
**Caption:** TABLE 6 Soil water balance in conventional vs. sod based rotational productions during 2019-2022 growing season.
![Table 6](tables/table_006_p16.png)
<!-- name: table_006_p16 -->

Analysis of: table_006_p16.png
================================================================================

## Content Type: Table

## Short Description:
The table presents a comprehensive soil water balance analysis for various crop rotations and conditions during the 2019-2022 growing seasons. It compares different rotational productions, including conventional versus sod-based systems, across multiple parameters such as rainfall, irrigation, evapotranspiration (ET), runoff, deep percolation, soil water storage, crop yield, and water productivity.

## Detailed Analysis:

### Table Purpose:
The table measures and compares the soil water balance in conventional versus sod-based rotational productions during the 2019-2022 growing seasons.

### Structure:
- **Column Headers:**
  - Rotation
  - Crop
  - Rainfall (mm)
  - Irrigation (mm)
  - ET (mm)
  - Runoff (mm)
  - Deep percolation (mm)
  - Soil water storage (∆S) (mm)
  - Yield (Kg ha⁻¹)
  - Crop water productivity (WPₑ-ETc) (kg m⁻³)
  - Water productivity (WPₑ-TWU) (kg m⁻³)

- **Row Labels:** 
  - Different crop rotations and conditions: PC, CP, CAP, BBCP, BBCAP, each with various crops like Peanut, Maize, Carrot, and Bahiagrass.

- **Units of Measurement:**
  - Rainfall, Irrigation, ET, Runoff, Deep percolation, Soil water storage: mm
  - Yield: Kg ha⁻¹
  - Crop water productivity, Water productivity: kg m⁻³

### Numerical Data & Relationships:
- **Key Numerical Values:**
  • **Maximum Yield:** 86119 Kg ha⁻¹ for Carrot under BBCAP
  • **Minimum Yield:** 5628 Kg ha⁻¹ for Peanut under CP
  • **Maximum Crop Water Productivity:** 28.61 kg m⁻³ for Carrot under BBCAP
  • **Minimum Crop Water Productivity:** 1.02 kg m⁻³ for Peanut under BBCP
  • **Maximum Water Productivity (WPₑ-TWU):** 13.98 kg m⁻³ for Carrot under BBCAP
  • **Minimum Water Productivity (WPₑ-TWU):** 0.69 kg m⁻³ for Peanut under CP

- **Relationships & Correlations:**
  - Generally, as rainfall increases, irrigation decreases, and vice versa, to meet the crop water requirements.
  - High ET values correspond with high crop yields, indicating that crops with higher water consumption tend to have better growth.
  - Soil water storage (∆S) varies significantly across different rotations and crops, influencing water productivity.

### Comparisons:
- **Crop Comparison:**
  - Maize and Peanut have multiple entries across different rotations, showing varied responses in yield and water productivity.
  - Carrot shows exceptionally high yield and water productivity under BBCAP.
"""

Rotation include: PC, peanut-maize; CP, maize-peanut; CAP, maize-carrot-peanut; BBCP, bahiagrass-bahiagrass-maize-peanut; BBCAP, bahiagrass-bahiagrass-maize-carrot-peanut.

# 3.5 Yield variability

The impact of crop rotation systems on maize, peanuts, and carrots yields in 2021 and 2022 crop growing season is presented in Figure 9. In 2021, maize yields varied signi /uniFB01 cantly between the conventional and sod-based rotations, with a p-value of 0.048 and a least signi /uniFB01 cant difference of 1408 kg ha -1 , indicating that conventional rotation may offer short-term bene /uniFB01 ts within a fouryear cycle (Bullock, 1992; Porter et al., 2003). In contrast, yields from the 2022 peanut and carrot growing seasons showed no signi /uniFB01 cant differences between rotations, with p-values of 0.057 and 0.08, respectively, suggesting that rotational effects could be crop-speci /uniFB01 c (Smith et al., 2008). Throughout the 2021 maize and 2022 peanut and carrot growing periods, irrigation and fertilizer inputs remained consistent across all rotations, emphasizing that yield differences were likely due to rotational practices rather than external inputs (Doucet et al., 1999).

Throughout the study, average yields were documented for each crop type under both conventional and sod-based rotations. For maize, the average yield in conventional rotations (PC, CP, and CAP) was 14,879 kg ha -1 with a standard deviation (SD) of 809 kg ha -1 , while yields in sod-based rotations (BBCP and BBCAP) averaged 13,755 kg ha -1 (SD: 1,010 kg ha -1 ). Peanut yields were 6,651 kg ha -1 (SD: 985 kg ha -1 ) under conventional rotation and 6,073 kg ha -1 (SD: 951 kg ha -1 ) in sod-based rotation. For carrots, the average yield was 77.9 Mg ha -1 (SD: 4.6 Mg ha -1 ) in conventional rotation (CAP) and 86.1 Mg ha -1 (SD: 6.5 Mg ha -1 ) in sod-based rotation (BBCAP). Findings indicate that adding a two-year bahiagrass phase to conventional rotations did not increase maize and peanut yields, suggesting a nuanced relationship between sod-based rotations and crop productivity. These results highlight the need for further research extending beyond the four-year study to better understand the long-term effects on sustainable farming (Porter et al., 2003).

# 3.6 Crop water production functions
